title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: members, contributors, leaders pledge make participation community harassment-free experience everyone, regardless age, body size, visible invisible disability, ethnicity, sex characteristics, gender identity expression, level experience, education, socio-economic status, nationality, personal appearance, race, caste, color, religion, sexual identity orientation. pledge act interact ways contribute open, welcoming, diverse, inclusive, healthy community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Examples behavior contributes positive environment community include: Demonstrating empathy kindness toward people respectful differing opinions, viewpoints, experiences Giving gracefully accepting constructive feedback Accepting responsibility apologizing affected mistakes, learning experience Focusing best just us individuals, overall community Examples unacceptable behavior include: use sexualized language imagery, sexual attention advances kind Trolling, insulting derogatory comments, personal political attacks Public private harassment Publishing others’ private information, physical email address, without explicit permission conduct reasonably considered inappropriate professional setting

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community leaders responsible clarifying enforcing standards acceptable behavior take appropriate fair corrective action response behavior deem inappropriate, threatening, offensive, harmful. Community leaders right responsibility remove, edit, reject comments, commits, code, wiki edits, issues, contributions aligned Code Conduct, communicate reasons moderation decisions appropriate.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Code Conduct applies within community spaces, also applies individual officially representing community public spaces. Examples representing community include using official e-mail address, posting via official social media account, acting appointed representative online offline event.

title: Contributing to fuzzystring
text: want make bigger change, ’s good idea first file issue make sure someone team agrees ’s needed. ’ve found bug, please file issue illustrates bug minimal reprex (also help write unit test, needed). See guide create great issue advice.

title: Contributing to fuzzystring
text: Fork package clone onto computer. haven’t done , recommend using usethis::create_from_github("PaulESantos/fuzzystring", fork = TRUE). Install development dependencies devtools::install_dev_deps(), make sure package passes R CMD check running devtools::check(). R CMD check doesn’t pass cleanly, ’s good idea ask help continuing. Create Git branch pull request (PR). recommend using usethis::pr_init("brief-description--change"). Make changes, commit git, create PR running usethis::pr_push(), following prompts browser. title PR briefly describe change. body PR contain Fixes #issue-number. user-facing changes, add bullet top NEWS.md (.e. just first header). Follow style described https://style.tidyverse.org/news.html.

title: Contributing to fuzzystring
text: New code follow tidyverse style guide. can use Air apply style, please don’t restyle code nothing PR. use roxygen2, Markdown syntax, documentation. use testthat unit tests. Contributions test cases included easier accept.

title: Contributing to fuzzystring
text: Please note fuzzystring project released Contributor Code Conduct. contributing project agree abide terms.

title: Benchmarking fuzzystring against fuzzyjoin
text: vignette documents benchmark used compare fuzzyjoin fuzzystring_join() reimplementation fuzzystring. benchmark code shown reproducibility, executed vignette built. Instead, analysis reads precomputed CSV snapshot bundled inst/extdata/rbase_string_benchmark.csv. benchmark covers 10 distance methods, 3 sample sizes (250, 500, 750), 10 repetitions per implementation. Across 30 method-size combinations, fuzzystring faster every case. mean runtime ratio 3.94x, meaning , average, fuzzyjoin takes almost four times long reimplemented fuzzystring path benchmark snapshot.

title: Benchmarking fuzzystring against fuzzyjoin
text: script benchmark used generate CSV analyzed document. displayed reference .
code: library(microbenchmark) library(fuzzyjoin) library(fuzzystring) library(qdapDictionaries) library(tibble)  samp_sizes <- c(250, 500, 750) seed <- 2016  params <- list(   list(method = "osa",     mode = "inner", max_dist = 1,   q = 0),   list(method = "lv",      mode = "inner", max_dist = 1,   q = 0),   list(method = "dl",      mode = "inner", max_dist = 1,   q = 0),   list(method = "hamming", mode = "inner", max_dist = 1,   q = 0),   list(method = "lcs",     mode = "inner", max_dist = 1,   q = 0),   list(method = "qgram",   mode = "inner", max_dist = 2,   q = 2),   list(method = "cosine",  mode = "inner", max_dist = 0.5, q = 2),   list(method = "jaccard", mode = "inner", max_dist = 0.5, q = 2),   list(method = "jw",      mode = "inner", max_dist = 0.5, q = 0),   list(method = "soundex", mode = "inner", max_dist = 0.5, q = 0) )  args <- commandArgs(trailingOnly = TRUE) if (length(args) > 0) {   params <- Filter(function(p) p$method %in% args, params) }  data(misspellings) words <- as.data.frame(DICTIONARY)  results <- data.frame()  for (p in params) {   for (nsamp in samp_sizes) {     cat(sprintf("Running %s with %d samples\n", p$method, nsamp))      set.seed(seed)     sub_misspellings <- misspellings[sample(seq_len(nrow(misspellings)), nsamp), ]      bench <- microbenchmark(       fuzzyjoin = {         fuzzy_res <- stringdist_join(           sub_misspellings, words,           by         = c(misspelling = "word"),           method     = p$method,           mode       = p$mode,           max_dist   = p$max_dist,           q          = p$q         )       },       fuzzystring = {         fuzzystring_res <- fuzzystring_join(           sub_misspellings, words,           by       = c(misspelling = "word"),           method   = p$method,           mode     = p$mode,           max_dist = p$max_dist,           q        = p$q         )       },       times = 10     )      # Verify that both implementations return the same result.     fuzzy_res <- stringdist_join(       sub_misspellings, words,       by       = c(misspelling = "word"),       method   = p$method,       mode     = p$mode,       max_dist = p$max_dist,       q        = p$q     )      fuzzystring_res <- fuzzystring_join(       sub_misspellings, words,       by       = c(misspelling = "word"),       method   = p$method,       mode     = p$mode,       max_dist = p$max_dist,       q        = p$q     )      if (!isTRUE(all.equal(fuzzy_res, fuzzystring_res)) && p$method != "soundex") {       message("Mismatch detected: fuzzyjoin vs fuzzystring for method: ", p$method)     }      df <- as.data.frame(bench)     df$method <- p$method     df$n_comps <- nrow(sub_misspellings) * nrow(words)     df$os <- unname(Sys.info()["sysname"])      results <- rbind(results, df)   } }

title: Benchmarking fuzzystring against fuzzyjoin
text: benchmark spans 10 string distance methods 3 sample sizes, producing 30 aggregated method-size combinations. table reports mean runtime milliseconds relative runtime ratio used throughout figures. ranking summarizes average speedup method. Larger values mean fuzzystring faster wider margin.

title: Benchmarking fuzzystring against fuzzyjoin
code: ggplot(   absolute_plot_data,   aes(x = comparisons_label, y = time_seconds, fill = implementation) ) +   geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.75), width = 0.65) +   facet_wrap(~ method_label, scales = "free_y") +   labs(     title = "Mean runtime by distance method",     x = "Candidate comparisons (millions)",     y = "Mean runtime (seconds)",     fill = "Implementation"   ) +   scale_fill_manual(     values = c("fuzzyjoin" = "#D95F02", "fuzzystring" = "#1B9E77"),     labels = c("fuzzyjoin", "fuzzystring")   ) +   scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) sprintf("%.1f", x)) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +   theme(     strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),     legend.position = "bottom",     plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),     plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),     axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1)   )

title: Benchmarking fuzzystring against fuzzyjoin
text: Values 1 indicate fuzzystring faster fuzzyjoin method workload size.
code: ggplot(   wide_summary,   aes(x = comparisons_label, y = runtime_ratio) ) +   geom_col(fill = "#D95F02", width = 0.55) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +   facet_wrap(~ method_label, scales = "free_y") +   labs(     title = "Relative runtime against fuzzystring",     subtitle = "Values above 1 mean fuzzystring is faster than fuzzyjoin",     x = "Candidate comparisons (millions)",     y = "Runtime ratio (fuzzyjoin / fuzzystring)"   ) +   scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) sprintf("%.1f", x)) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +   theme(     strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),     legend.position = "none",     plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),     plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),     axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1)   )

title: Benchmarking fuzzystring against fuzzyjoin
text: benchmark snapshot supports conclusion across methods tested: fuzzystring reimplementation consistently faster fuzzyjoin workloads covered . largest average gains appear Hamming, Levenshtein, OSA, Damerau-Levenshtein, cosine, q-gram, jaccard, soundex still show clear smaller improvement. words, reimplemented path match fuzzyjoin functionally; also improves runtime across every benchmarked method-size combination snapshot.

title: Getting Started with fuzzystring
text: fuzzystring provides fast, flexible fuzzy string joins data.frame data.table objects using approximate string matching. Built top data.table stringdist, uses compiled C++ result assembly plus adaptive candidate planning reduce unnecessary distance evaluations single-column joins.

title: Getting Started with fuzzystring
text: can install fuzzystring CRAN: can also install development version GitHub:
code: install.packages("fuzzystring") # Using pak (recommended) # pak::pak("PaulESantos/fuzzystring")  # Or using remotes # remotes::install_github("PaulESantos/fuzzystring")

title: Getting Started with fuzzystring
text: ’s simple example matching diamond cuts slight misspellings:
code: # Your messy data x <- data.frame(   name = c("Idea", "Premiom", "Very Good"),    id = 1:3 )  # Reference data y <- data.frame(   approx_name = c("Ideal", "Premium", "VeryGood"),    grp = c("A", "B", "C") )  # Fuzzy join with max distance of 2 edits fuzzystring_inner_join(   x, y,   by = c(name = "approx_name"),   max_dist = 2,   distance_col = "distance" ) #>        name id approx_name grp distance #> 1      Idea  1       Ideal   A        1 #> 2   Premiom  2     Premium   B        1 #> 3 Very Good  3    VeryGood   C        1

title: Getting Started with fuzzystring
text: fuzzystring supports standard join types. small, reusable example dataset can compare behavior join family. fuzzystring_inner_join(): matching rows. fuzzystring_left_join(): rows x, matching rows y. fuzzystring_right_join(): rows y, matching rows x. fuzzystring_full_join(): rows tables. fuzzystring_semi_join(): Rows x match y. fuzzystring_anti_join(): Rows x don’t match y.
code: x_join <- data.frame(   name = c("Idea", "Premiom", "Very Good", "Gooood"),   id = 1:4 )  y_join <- data.frame(   approx_name = c("Ideal", "Premium", "VeryGood", "Good"),   grp = c("A", "B", "C", "D") )

title: Getting Started with fuzzystring
text: prefer single entry point, can use fuzzystring_join() directly specifying mode.
code: fuzzystring_join(   x_join, y_join,   by = c(name = "approx_name"),   max_dist = 2,   mode = "left",   distance_col = "distance" ) #>        name id approx_name grp distance #> 1      Idea  1       Ideal   A        1 #> 2   Premiom  2     Premium   B        1 #> 3 Very Good  3    VeryGood   C        1 #> 4    Gooood  4        Good   D        2

title: Getting Started with fuzzystring
text: can choose various distance metrics provided stringdist package:
code: # Optimal String Alignment (default) fuzzystring_inner_join(x, y, by = c(name = "approx_name"), method = "osa")  # Damerau-Levenshtein fuzzystring_inner_join(x, y, by = c(name = "approx_name"), method = "dl")  # Jaro-Winkler (good for names) fuzzystring_inner_join(x, y, by = c(name = "approx_name"), method = "jw")  # Soundex (phonetic matching) fuzzystring_inner_join(x, y, by = c(name = "approx_name"), method = "soundex")

title: Getting Started with fuzzystring
text: Use ignore_case = TRUE ignore capitalization:
code: fuzzystring_inner_join(   x, y,    by = c(name = "approx_name"),   ignore_case = TRUE,   max_dist = 1 )

title: Getting Started with fuzzystring
text: can match multiple string columns . distance method threshold applied mapped column.
code: x_multi <- data.frame(   first = c("Jon", "Maira"),   last = c("Smyth", "Gonzales") )  y_multi <- data.frame(   first_ref = c("John", "Maria"),   last_ref = c("Smith", "Gonzalez"),   customer_id = 1:2 )  fuzzystring_inner_join(   x_multi, y_multi,   by = c(first = "first_ref", last = "last_ref"),   method = "osa",   max_dist = 1 )

title: Getting Started with fuzzystring
text: fuzzystring now keeps join execution compiled C++ path using data.table orchestrate candidate generation. practice means compiled row expansion binding across join modes, better preservation typed columns, adaptive candidate planning helps duplicate-heavy low-duplication workloads. dedicated comparison fuzzyjoin::stringdist_join(), see benchmark article bundled package.

what: Fast Fuzzy String Joins for Data Frames
title: Fast Fuzzy String Joins for Data Frames
text: fuzzystring provides fast, flexible fuzzy string joins data.frame data.table objects using approximate string matching. combines stringdist-based matching data.table backend compiled C++ result assembly reduce overhead large joins preserving standard join semantics.

title: Fast Fuzzy String Joins for Data Frames
text: Real-world identifiers rarely line exactly. fuzzystring designed workloads : matching customer company names typos reconciling product catalogs inconsistent labels linking survey responses controlled vocabulary joining reference tables messy user input package includes: fuzzy inner, left, right, full, semi, anti joins multiple stringdist methods, including OSA, Levenshtein, Damerau-Levenshtein, Jaro-Winkler, q-gram, cosine, jaccard, soundex output preserves class x (data.table, tibble, base data.frame) optional distance columns matched pairs case-insensitive matching adaptive candidate planning single-column joins compiled C++ row expansion result assembly across join modes

title: Fast Fuzzy String Joins for Data Frames
code: # Install from CRAN install.packages("fuzzystring")  # Development version from GitHub # pak::pak("PaulESantos/fuzzystring") # remotes::install_github("PaulESantos/fuzzystring")

title: Fast Fuzzy String Joins for Data Frames
code: library(fuzzystring)  x <- data.frame(   name = c("Idea", "Premiom", "Very Good"),   id = 1:3 )  y <- data.frame(   approx_name = c("Ideal", "Premium", "VeryGood"),   grp = c("A", "B", "C") )  fuzzystring_inner_join(   x, y,   by = c(name = "approx_name"),   max_dist = 2,   distance_col = "distance" )

title: Fast Fuzzy String Joins for Data Frames
code: fuzzystring_inner_join(x, y, by = c(name = "approx_name"), max_dist = 2) fuzzystring_left_join(x, y, by = c(name = "approx_name"), max_dist = 2) fuzzystring_right_join(x, y, by = c(name = "approx_name"), max_dist = 2) fuzzystring_full_join(x, y, by = c(name = "approx_name"), max_dist = 2) fuzzystring_semi_join(x, y, by = c(name = "approx_name"), max_dist = 2) fuzzystring_anti_join(x, y, by = c(name = "approx_name"), max_dist = 2)

code: fuzzystring_inner_join(x, y, by = c(name = "approx_name"), method = "osa") fuzzystring_inner_join(x, y, by = c(name = "approx_name"), method = "dl") fuzzystring_inner_join(x, y, by = c(name = "approx_name"), method = "jw") fuzzystring_inner_join(x, y, by = c(name = "approx_name"), method = "soundex")

text: fuzzystring keeps join execution compiled path original fuzzyjoin implementation. practice, package combines: data.table grouping candidate planning adaptive blocking single-column string joins compiled row expansion, row binding, final assembly type-preserving handling dates, datetimes, factors, list-columns benchmark article summarizes precomputed comparison fuzzyjoin::stringdist_join() using methods sample sizes: Getting started: https://paulesantos.github.io/fuzzystring/articles/getting_started.html Benchmark article: https://paulesantos.github.io/fuzzystring/articles/benchmark_fuzzyjoin_comparison.html

title: Fast Fuzzy String Joins for Data Frames
text: fuzzystring_join() can match across one string column applying distance method threshold mapped column.
code: x_multi <- data.frame(   first = c("Jon", "Maira"),   last = c("Smyth", "Gonzales") )  y_multi <- data.frame(   first_ref = c("John", "Maria"),   last_ref = c("Smith", "Gonzalez"),   id = 1:2 )  fuzzystring_inner_join(   x_multi, y_multi,   by = c(first = "first_ref", last = "last_ref"),   method = "osa",   max_dist = 1 )

title: Fast Fuzzy String Joins for Data Frames
text: fuzzyjoin: original fuzzy join API inspired package stringdist: distance metrics data.table: high-performance tabular backend

text: fuzzystring builds ideas popularized fuzzyjoin, reinterpreting join pipeline around data.table compiled C++ result assembly.

what: fuzzystring: Fast fuzzy string joins for data frames — fuzzystring
title: fuzzystring: Fast fuzzy string joins for data frames — fuzzystring
text: fuzzystring provides fuzzy inner, left, right, full, semi, anti joins data.frame data.table objects using approximate string matching. combines stringdist metrics data.table backend compiled C++ result assembly reduce overhead large joins preserving familiar join semantics.

title: fuzzystring: Fast fuzzy string joins for data frames — fuzzystring
text: Main entry points fuzzystring_join() convenience wrappers fuzzystring_inner_join(), fuzzystring_left_join(), fuzzystring_right_join(), fuzzystring_full_join(), fuzzystring_semi_join(), fuzzystring_anti_join(). package also includes example dataset misspellings.

title: fuzzystring: Fast fuzzy string joins for data frames — fuzzystring
text: Maintainer: Paul E. Santos Andrade paulefrens@gmail.com (ORCID) contributors: David Robinson admiral.david@gmail.com (aut fuzzyjoin) [contributor]

what: Join two tables based on fuzzy string matching — fuzzystring_join
title: Join two tables based on fuzzy string matching — fuzzystring_join
text: Uses stringdist::stringdist() compute distances data.table-orchestrated backend compiled 'C++' assembly produce final result. main user-facing entry point fuzzy joins strings.

title: Join two tables based on fuzzy string matching — fuzzystring_join
code: fuzzystring_join(   x,   y,   by = NULL,   max_dist = 2,   method = c("osa", "lv", "dl", "hamming", "lcs", "qgram", "cosine", "jaccard", "jw",     "soundex"),   mode = "inner",   ignore_case = FALSE,   distance_col = NULL,   ... )  fuzzystring_inner_join(x, y, by = NULL, distance_col = NULL, ...)  fuzzystring_left_join(x, y, by = NULL, distance_col = NULL, ...)  fuzzystring_right_join(x, y, by = NULL, distance_col = NULL, ...)  fuzzystring_full_join(x, y, by = NULL, distance_col = NULL, ...)  fuzzystring_semi_join(x, y, by = NULL, distance_col = NULL, ...)  fuzzystring_anti_join(x, y, by = NULL, distance_col = NULL, ...)

title: Join two tables based on fuzzy string matching — fuzzystring_join
text: x data.frame data.table. y data.frame data.table. Columns join two tables. can supply character vector common names (e.g. c("name") ), named vector mapping x y (e.g. c(name = "approx_name")). max_dist Maximum distance use joining. Smaller values stricter. method Method computing string distance, see ?stringdist::stringdist stringdist package vignettes. mode One "inner", "left", "right", "full", "semi", "anti". ignore_case Logical; TRUE, comparisons case-insensitive. distance_col NULL, adds column name containing computed distance matched pair (NA unmatched rows outer joins). ... Additional arguments passed stringdist.

title: Join two tables based on fuzzy string matching — fuzzystring_join
text: joined table preserves container class x: data.table inputs return data.table, tibble inputs return tibble, plain data.frame inputs return plain data.frame. See fuzzystring_join_backend details output structure.

title: Join two tables based on fuzzy string matching — fuzzystring_join
text: method = "soundex", max_dist automatically set 0.5, since Soundex distance 0 (match) 1 (match). maps multiple columns, method, max_dist, additional stringdist arguments applied independently mapped column, row pair kept mapped columns satisfy distance threshold. single-column joins, fuzzystring uses adaptive candidate planning calling stringdist::stringdist(). Levenshtein-like methods ("osa", "lv", "dl"), fast prefilter applied: abs(nchar(v1) - nchar(v2)) > max_dist, pair match, distance computed pair. low-duplication workloads, planner can also evaluate larger dense blocks unique values reduce orchestration overhead preserving matching semantics.

title: Join two tables based on fuzzy string matching — fuzzystring_join
code: # \donttest{ if (requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)) {   d <- data.table::data.table(approximate_name = c("Idea", "Premiom"))   # Match diamonds$cut to d$approximate_name   res <- fuzzystring_inner_join(ggplot2::diamonds, d,     by = c(cut = "approximate_name"),     max_dist = 1   )   head(res) } #> # A tibble: 6 × 11 #>   carat cut   color clarity depth table price     x     y     z approximate_name #>   <dbl> <ord> <ord> <ord>   <dbl> <dbl> <int> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr>            #> 1  0.23 Ideal E     SI2      61.5    55   326  3.95  3.98  2.43 Idea             #> 2  0.21 Prem… E     SI1      59.8    61   326  3.89  3.84  2.31 Premiom          #> 3  0.29 Prem… I     VS2      62.4    58   334  4.2   4.23  2.63 Premiom          #> 4  0.23 Ideal J     VS1      62.8    56   340  3.93  3.9   2.46 Idea             #> 5  0.22 Prem… F     SI1      60.4    61   342  3.88  3.84  2.33 Premiom          #> 6  0.31 Ideal J     SI2      62.2    54   344  4.35  4.37  2.71 Idea             # }

title: Fuzzy join backend using 'data.table' plus compiled 'C++' assembly — fuzzystring_join_backend
code: fuzzystring_join_backend(   x,   y,   by = NULL,   match_fun = NULL,   multi_by = NULL,   multi_match_fun = NULL,   index_match_fun = NULL,   mode = "inner",   ... )

title: Fuzzy join backend using 'data.table' plus compiled 'C++' assembly — fuzzystring_join_backend
text: x data.frame data.table. y data.frame data.table. Columns join two tables. See fuzzystring_join. match_fun function used match values. must return logical vector (data.frame/data.table whose first column logical) indicating pairs match. multi-column joins, may pass list functions (one per column). multi_by character vector column names used multi-column matching multi_match_fun supplied. multi_match_fun function receives matrices unique values x y (rows correspond unique combinations multi_by). must return logical vector (data.frame/data.table whose first column logical) indicating rows match. index_match_fun function receives joined columns x y returns table integer columns x y (1-based row indices). mode One "inner", "left", "right", "full", "semi", "anti". ... Additional arguments passed matching function(s).

title: Fuzzy join backend using 'data.table' plus compiled 'C++' assembly — fuzzystring_join_backend
text: joined table preserves container class x: data.table inputs return data.table, tibble inputs return tibble, plain data.frame inputs return plain data.frame. See fuzzystring_join.

title: Fuzzy join backend using 'data.table' plus compiled 'C++' assembly — fuzzystring_join_backend
text: function works like fuzzystring_join, replaces R-based output assembly compiled 'C++' implementation. provides better performance, especially large joins, outer joins, wide tables. intended backend compute distances ; use fuzzystring_join string-distance based matching. C++ implementation handles: Efficient subsetting row indices Proper handling NA values outer joins Type-safe column operations common R types Preservation factor levels attributes Column name conflicts .x/.y suffixes

title: A corpus of common misspellings, for examples and practice — misspellings
code: # \donttest{ library(rvest) library(readr) #>  #> Attaching package: ‘readr’ #> The following object is masked from ‘package:rvest’: #>  #>     guess_encoding library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: ‘dplyr’ #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:stats’: #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:base’: #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union library(stringr) library(tidyr)  u <- "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Lists_of_common_misspellings/For_machines" h <- read_html(u)  misspellings <- h %>%   html_nodes("pre") %>%   html_text() %>%   read_delim(col_names = c("misspelling", "correct"),                     delim = ">",                     skip = 1) %>%   mutate(misspelling = str_sub(misspelling,                                                1, -2)) |>   separate_rows(correct, sep = ", ") |>   filter(Encoding(correct) != "UTF-8") #> Warning: The `file` argument of `read_delim()` should use `I()` for literal data as of #> readr 2.2.0. #>    #>   # Bad (for example): #>   read_csv("x,y\n1,2") #>    #>   # Good: #>   read_csv(I("x,y\n1,2")) #> Rows: 4309 Columns: 2 #> ── Column specification ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Delimiter: ">" #> chr (2): misspelling, correct #>  #> ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data. #> ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message. # }

title: fuzzystring 0.0.3
text: Refined package metadata pkgdown/homepage descriptions current data.table plus compiled C++ backend. Reworked README match public API added direct links getting-started benchmark articles. Added benchmark vignette comparing fuzzystring fuzzyjoin precomputed CSV snapshot shipped inst/extdata/. Updated vignette text reflect current implementation installation paths.
